evidence_text: The study evaluates 180 controlled agent-system configurations across four benchmarks, five architectures, and three LLM families while standardizing tools, prompts, and token budgets.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, controlled evaluation setup
source_excerpt: “We evaluate this across four diverse benchmarks... Using five canonical agent architectures... instantiated across three LLM families, we perform a controlled evaluation spanning 180 configurations, standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets to isolate architectural effects from implementation confounds.”
note: Controlled setup is presented as the basis for attributing observed differences to architecture rather than prompt or tooling differences.
caveat: Controlled setup is presented as the basis for attributing observed differences to architecture rather than prompt or tooling differences.

evidence_text: The predictive framework achieves cross-validated R² = 0.513 and predicts the optimal coordination strategy for 87% of held-out configurations.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, predictive model performance
source_excerpt: “We derive a predictive model using empirical coordination metrics... that achieves cross-validated R2 = 0.513... The framework predicts the optimal coordination strategy for 87% of held-out configurations.”
note: The claim is about predictive performance on held-out configurations, not universal correctness.
caveat: The claim is about predictive performance on held-out configurations, not universal correctness.

evidence_text: Tool-heavy tasks show a negative coordination interaction, with β = -0.330 and p < 0.001.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, tool-coordination trade-off
source_excerpt: “Our analysis identifies three patterns. First, a tool-coordination trade-off (β = −0.330, p < 0.001): tool-heavy tasks... suffer from multi-agent coordination overhead, with efficiency penalties compounding as environmental complexity increases.”
note: The source directly links lower performance in tool-heavy settings to coordination overhead.
caveat: The source directly links lower performance in tool-heavy settings to coordination overhead.

evidence_text: When single-agent baseline performance exceeds about 45%, additional agents show diminishing or negative returns, with β = -0.408 and p < 0.001.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, capability ceiling
source_excerpt: “Second, a capability ceiling (β = −0.408, p < 0.001): tasks where single-agent performance already exceeds 45% accuracy experience negative returns from additional agents, as coordination costs exceed diminishing improvement potential.”
note: This is a boundary condition on multi-agent gains: more coordination is not presented as universally helpful once single-agent performance is already relatively high.
caveat: This is a boundary condition on multi-agent gains: more coordination is not presented as universally helpful once single-agent performance is already relatively high.

evidence_text: Independent multi-agent systems amplify errors 17.2× relative to single-agent baseline, while centralized coordination contains error amplification to 4.4×.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, error amplification by architecture
source_excerpt: “Third, architecture-dependent error amplification where independent multi-agent systems amplify errors 17.2-fold versus single-agent baseline through unchecked error propagation... while centralized coordination achieves 4.4-fold containment via validation bottlenecks...”
note: The source frames the architecture difference as a mechanism: independent systems lack inter-agent verification, while centralized systems use an orchestrator bottleneck to catch errors.
caveat: The source frames the architecture difference as a mechanism: independent systems lack inter-agent verification, while centralized systems use an orchestrator bottleneck to catch errors.

evidence_text: On Finance Agent, centralized multi-agent coordination improves mean performance by 80.9%, from 0.349 for single-agent to 0.631.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Finance Agent gains
source_excerpt: “On Finance Agent, MAS achieve substantial improvements: Centralized reaches +80.9% (mean 0.631 vs. SAS 0.349)...”
note: This is benchmark-specific evidence for a large positive multi-agent effect in a structured financial reasoning task.
caveat: This is benchmark-specific evidence for a large positive multi-agent effect in a structured financial reasoning task.

evidence_text: On BrowseComp-Plus, decentralized coordination improves performance by 9.2% over single-agent, while centralized coordination is nearly flat at +0.2%.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, BrowseComp-Plus architecture comparison
source_excerpt: “On BrowseComp-Plus, improvements remain modest: Decentralized achieves +9.2% (0.347 vs. SAS 0.318), with Centralized essentially flat at +0.2%.”
note: The source presents this as task-contingent architecture behavior rather than a general multi-agent advantage.
caveat: The source presents this as task-contingent architecture behavior rather than a general multi-agent advantage.

evidence_text: On PlanCraft, every tested multi-agent architecture underperforms the single-agent baseline, with degradations ranging from 39.0% to 70.0%.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, PlanCraft degradation
source_excerpt:
“Critically, PlanCraft exhibits universal performance degradation across all multi-agent architectures... Hybrid to -39.0%... and Independent to -70.0%.”
Also: “all multi-agent variants universally degrade performance on tasks requiring sequential constraint satisfaction (planning: -39% to -70%).”
note: This is a direct counterexample to claims that adding agents reliably improves performance.
caveat: This is a direct counterexample to claims that adding agents reliably improves performance.

evidence_text: Total reasoning turns scale with agent count as T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724 with R² = 0.974.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, turn-count scaling
source_excerpt: “Total reasoning turns... exhibit power-law growth with agent count: T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, R2 = 0.974, 95% CI on exponent: [1.685,1.763], p < 0.001.”
note: The source uses this super-linear scaling result to argue that communication cost rises quickly as team size increases.
caveat: The source uses this super-linear scaling result to argue that communication cost rises quickly as team size increases.

evidence_text: Average coordination overhead is reported as 58% for Independent, 263% for Decentralized, 285% for Centralized, and 515% for Hybrid, relative to single-agent.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, coordination overhead by architecture
source_excerpt: “Table 5... Overhead (O%)... SAS 0, Independent 58, Decentralized 263, Centralized 285, Hybrid 515.”
note: These values are descriptive architecture-level metrics and indicate large cost differences even before task-specific performance is considered.
caveat: These values are descriptive architecture-level metrics and indicate large cost differences even before task-specific performance is considered.

evidence_text: Centralized, hybrid, and decentralized architectures achieve an average 22.7% factual-error reduction, while independent multi-agent systems show 4.6% error amplification.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, error absorption
source_excerpt: “These architectures achieve 22.7% average error reduction (95% CI: [20.1%, 25.3%])... Independent MAS shows no error correction (+4.6% amplification) due to absence of any inter-agent verification mechanism...”
note: The evidence ties lower error rates to iterative verification, and it separately states that lack of verification in independent systems leads to amplification instead of correction.
caveat: The evidence ties lower error rates to iterative verification, and it separately states that lack of verification in independent systems leads to amplification instead of correction.

evidence_text: The appendix defines a domain-complexity threshold near D ≈ 0.40, below which multi-agent systems tend to yield net positive returns and above which coordination overhead tends to dominate.
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, domain complexity threshold
source_excerpt: “We evaluate this across four diverse benchmarks... Using five canonical agent architectures... instantiated across three LLM families, we perform a controlled evaluation spanning 180 configurations, standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets to isolate architectural effects from implementation confounds.”
note: Controlled setup is presented as the basis for attributing observed differences to architecture rather than prompt or tooling differences.
caveat: Controlled setup is presented as the basis for attributing observed differences to architecture rather than prompt or tooling differences.
